title: Spectral analysis of equilibration: information leakage in isolated quantum systems
authors: André T. Cesário, Marcos G. Alpino, Reinaldo O. Vianna, Tiago Debarba
abstract: We develop a unified dynamical-spectral framework for equilibration in isolated quantum systems based on a subspace coarse-graining approach. Central to our formulation is the Leakage Fidelity Function (LFF), defined as the probability that a unitarily evolving state escapes the support of its initial subspace. This quantity provides a direct, operational measure of information flow and memory loss without invoking ensemble assumptions or perturbative arguments. We derive universal bounds on temporal fluctuations of the LFF, in terms of the spectral gap structure and the square of the effective dimension, evincing that large spectral delocalization suppresses fluctuations and guarantees equilibration on average. By introducing spectral power distributions and associated entropic measures, we establish a quantitative link between phase mixing, gap participation, and dynamical stability. We further investigate the equilibration timescale by connecting the LFF to quantum speed limits, thereby revealing the average time required for equilibration. Our results provide a state-dependent, geometrically transparent perspective on how spectral complexity and subspace information leakage jointly govern irreversibility in closed quantum many-body systems.
summary: This theoretical paper introduces the Leakage Fidelity Function (LFF) as a precise measure of information leakage during equilibration in closed quantum systems. It establishes that the dynamics are controlled not by a single parameter, but by a hierarchy of spectral descriptors—including dynamical and power spectral dimensions—which quantify how fluctuations decay over time.
main_problem: The paper develops a quantitative framework to measure equilibration in isolated quantum systems by analyzing how information leaks from an initial subspace over time.
main_result: Equilibration dynamics are governed by a hierarchy of spectral descriptors—ordinary, dynamical, and power spectral effective dimensions—which provide a state-dependent view of irreversibility.
method: The core method involves defining the Leakage Fidelity Function (LFF) and deriving universal bounds on its temporal fluctuations using spectral analysis techniques like Fourier transforms and entropy measures.
model_or_system: The theory is applied to isolated quantum systems evolving under unitary dynamics, analyzed via projection onto subspaces defined by an initial state's support. Specific examples include Ising-like chains and general many-body Hamiltonians.
key_observables: Leakage Fidelity Function (LFF), Leakage Coherence ($	ext{Cleak}$), Ordinary Effective Dimension ($d_{	ext{eff}}$), Dynamical Effective Dimension ($d_{	ext{dyn}}$), Power Spectral Entropy ($H_{	ext{pow}}$).
important_parameters: Spectral gap structure, effective dimension ($d_{	ext{eff}}$), time scale $T$, and the degree of spectral delocalization.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on deriving universal bounds applicable to fixed Hamiltonians; some parts assume Haar-typical state distributions or non-degenerate energy gaps for specific theorems.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate LFF behavior showing small fluctuations for equilibrating systems versus large oscillations for quasi-periodic ones. Later figures compare the growth patterns of $	ext{deff}$, $	ext{ddyn}$, and $	ext{dspec}$ using synthetic state distributions to separate their physical roles.
paper_structure: The paper builds from defining the LFF as an operational measure, deriving universal bounds on its variance (Theorem 1), connecting it to spectral properties ($d_{	ext{eff}}$), and finally introducing a hierarchy of descriptors ($	ext{ddyn}, 	ext{dspec}$) to show that fluctuation dynamics are richer than simple state-space measures.
why_interesting: This work provides powerful, quantitative diagnostics for open quantum systems theory by moving beyond simple time averages. The distinction between different effective dimensions offers new tools to characterize the rate and nature of thermalization in complex many-body models.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Illustrative Image of Leakage Fidelity. In both panels, the floor illustrates the state distribution within the Hilbert space H = H0 ⊕H⊥. The side wall represents the spectral power density GL(ω) = |δL (ω)|2 as a function of the Hamiltonian spectrum, in an energy shell ω. The front wall shows the Leakage Fidelity Function L (t) as a function of time. Panel a) illustrates an isolated equilibrating system, where ρ(t) ∈H0 ⊕H⊥for all times t →T. In this case, the spectral power density GL(ω) spreads over the entire energy spectrum, and the LFF signal δL (t) = |L (t) −⟨L (t)⟩T | exhibits small fluctuations around its average equilibrium value. Panel b) illustrates a quasi- periodic...

Figure 2: Time evolution of L0(t) for three different initial states, together with the corresponding running time- averaged quadratic deviation from equilibrium shown in the inset, δL0(t) = L0(t) −L ∞ 0 . The all spin up state (Up = |↑· · · ↑⟩) exhibits large and persistent oscillations, indicating stronger nonequilibrium fluctuations throughout the dynamics. By contrast, down (Dw = |↓· · · ↓⟩) and paramagnetic (Pm = |↑↓· · · ↑↓⟩) state remain much closer to L ∞ 0 , displaying smoother behavior and a faster decay of the averaged fluctuation measure. This comparison makes explicit that states with similar energetic scales may nevertheless present markedly different dynamics, depending on...

Figure 5: Power entropy Hpow as a function of different effective dimensionalities for random quantum states gen- erated from an Ising-type Hamiltonian with N = 10 spins, corresponding to a Hilbert-space dimension dE = 210. A total of 5000 random population states were generated in the energy eigenbasis, producing a broad coverage of effective dimensions across the available state space. The red curve corresponds to the K-HUB model, defined by uniform population distributions over K energy levels, for which Hpow is computed analytically as a function of K ∈[2, dE].
